title: Why recovery is not about becoming who you were before
author: Kalesh
excerpt: I used to think recovery was about getting back to who I was before. Before the breakdown. Before the chronic hypervigilance. Before my nervous system learned...
metaDescription: I used to think recovery was about getting back to who I was before. Before the breakdown. Before the chronic hypervigilance. Before my nervous system lea...
htmlContent:
I used to think recovery was about getting back to who I was before. Before the breakdown. Before the chronic hypervigilance. Before my nervous system learned to treat safety as a threat. I spent years trying to excavate some original, undamaged version of myself. I thought if I just worked hard enough, I could find her and slip back into her skin. It never worked. And here's the thing: it was never supposed to.

Recovery from complex trauma isn't a restoration project. It's not about sanding off the rough edges of your experience until you look like you used to. That person you were before? They didn't know what you know now. They hadn't felt what you've felt. They hadn't learned the things your body learned in the dark. You can't go back to someone who hasn't lived your life. And honestly, why would you want to?

I know that question might sting. I know there's a part of you that still grieves for that younger self, the one who hadn't been broken open yet. I feel that grief too. But staying in that grief keeps you looking backward, and your healing is happening right here, in the present, in the body you're in right now.

The myth of the original self

When I first started reading about trauma recovery, I kept bumping into this idea that there was a "true self" somewhere underneath all the coping mechanisms and survival strategies. Peel back the layers of protection, the thinking went, and you'd find the real you, pristine and untouched. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, talks about how trauma actually reshapes your brain and your nervous system. It's not a layer you can peel away. It's a transformation that gets woven into your very biology.

Think about it. If your nervous system learned to scan for danger every second of every day, that's not a mask you're wearing. That's a fundamental adaptation. Your body learned to survive in an environment that wasn't safe. That learning is real. It's encoded in your vagus nerve, in your fight-or-flight responses, in the way your shoulders hunch and your breath gets shallow when someone raises their voice.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stuck in the body. It's not a story you can talk your way out of. It's a physiological event that needs to be completed, released, and integrated. And integration doesn't mean erasure. It means your system learns to include the trauma without being ruled by it. You become someone who carries their history without being crushed by it.

That's not who you were before. That's someone new. Someone with depth and resilience and a hard-won understanding of what it means to survive.

Why "before" was never the goal

I spent a long time romanticizing my past. I looked at old photos and thought, there she is, that's the real me. But here's what I wasn't seeing: that person also had struggles. She also had coping mechanisms. She also had parts of herself she hadn't met yet. The "before" version of me wasn't a finished product. She was just as messy and confused and human as I am now. She just hadn't been tested in the same way.

Recovery isn't about getting back to a baseline you've idealized. It's about building a new baseline, one that includes everything you've learned. One that has room for your grief and your anger and your joy, all at once. One that doesn't pretend the hard stuff didn't happen.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist who specializes in structural dissociation, often says that the goal isn't to get rid of your trauma responses. The goal is to build a relationship with them. To understand what each part of you is trying to do. To thank the hypervigilant part for keeping you alive, even if it's exhausting. To reassure the frozen part that it's safe to move now. You're not erasing those parts. You're integrating them into a more complete version of yourself.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you," and that shift alone can change how you see your own story. You stop looking for a broken version of yourself and start looking for a person who adapted to survive.

Stay with me here. I know this can feel like a loss. Letting go of the idea that you can go back to who you were before is a genuine grief. I've cried over it. I've raged at it. I've wished I could just be "normal" again, whatever that meant. But normal was never the point. Alive is the point. Present is the point. Connected is the point.

The shape of something new

So if recovery isn't about going back, what is it about? I think it's about becoming someone who can hold all of it. Someone who can feel the fear and the sadness and the rage without being swallowed by them. Someone who can let joy in without waiting for the other shoe to drop. Someone who can sit with discomfort and know it will pass.

This is the work of nervous system regulation. Deb Dana, who studied under Stephen Porges, writes about how we can train our vagus nerve to find safety in connection. It's not about never feeling triggered again. It's about building enough capacity in your system that you can come back to regulation more quickly. You don't have to be the person who never gets knocked down. You get to be the person who knows how to get back up.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Boundaries are part of this new shape you're building. They're not walls to keep everyone out. They're gates you get to open and close, based on what your nervous system needs. That's not something the "before" version of you knew how to do. That's something you're learning now.

And learning it changes you. It changes how you show up in relationships. It changes how you talk to yourself. It changes what you tolerate and what you walk away from. You're not the same person who couldn't say no. You're not the same person who stayed in rooms that weren't safe. You're someone who has practiced saying "this is where I end and you begin." That's not a regression. That's an evolution.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. He talks about the emotional flashbacks and the inner critic that come with complex trauma, and he offers practical steps for moving from survival mode into something that looks more like living. It's not about becoming who you were before. It's about becoming someone who can actually thrive, for the first time.

The grief and the gift

I'm not going to pretend this is easy. Letting go of the "before" self can feel like losing a friend. There's a part of you that still wants to go back to that simpler time, even if it wasn't actually simple. Even if it was just less complicated because you hadn't faced your trauma yet.

But here's what I've learned, sitting with my own grief: the person you're becoming is more real than the person you were. That "before" self was living with a lot of unconscious patterns, a lot of unexamined pain, a lot of coping mechanisms she didn't even know she had. She wasn't free. She was just unaware of her chains.

You, on the other hand, are waking up. You're seeing the chains. You're learning how to loosen them. You're building a life that has room for your full humanity, not just the parts that were acceptable or easy to be around. That's not a loss. That's a liberation.

Wild, right? The thing you thought was destruction turns out to be the doorway. The breakdown you survived becomes the foundation for something more honest. The parts of you you wanted to get rid of become the parts that teach you how to be whole.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz. It's about Internal Family Systems, and it's built on the idea that every part of you, even the ones you hate, has a positive intention. Even the part that keeps you small. Even the part that lashes out. Even the part that wants to give up. They're all trying to protect you. And when you can see them that way, you stop trying to exile them. You start trying to befriend them.

That's the work. Not becoming who you were. Becoming who you are, in all your complexity. Becoming someone who can hold the grief and the gratitude at the same time. Becoming someone who knows that healing isn't a straight line and that's okay.

Healing Journey Resources

  • What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry - A compassionate reframing of trauma that shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you."
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace by Nedra Glover Tawwab - A practical guide to building healthy boundaries that support your nervous system and your relationships.
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker - A resource for understanding and healing from complex trauma, with practical tools for managing flashbacks and the inner critic.
  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz - An introduction to Internal Family Systems that helps you befriend all the parts of yourself, even the ones you've tried to push away.
